publications.title: Molecular basis of potent antiviral HLA-C-restricted CD8<sup>+</sup> T cell response to an immunodominant SARS-CoV-2 nucleocapsid epitope.
publications.authors: Goto Y, Ahn YM, Toyoda M, Hamana H, Jin Y, Aritsu Y, Nakama T, Tajima Y, Maddumage JC, Li H, Kitamatsu M, Kishi H, Yonekawa A, Jayasinghe D, Shimono N, Nagasaki Y, Minami R, Toya T, Sekiya N, Tomita Y, Chatzileontiadou DSM, Nakata H, Nakagawa S, Sakagami T, Ueno T, Gras S, Motozono C.
publications.executive_summary: The study characterizes an immunodominant HLA-C*12:02-restricted CD8+ T cell response to SARS-CoV-2 nucleocapsid protein in COVID-19 convalescent donors, focusing on the KF9 peptide (N266-274: KAYNVTQAF) prevalent in the HLA-A24-B52-C12 haplotype common in Japan. KF9-specific T cells demonstrate potent cytotoxicity and suppress viral replication across both ancestral and multiple variants of concern, while maintaining stable effector memory and rapid recall responses for at least 12 months post-infection. The crystal/cryo-EM structure of the TCR-HLA-C*12:02-KF9 complex reveals an unexpected mechanism: despite high TCR affinity, the CDR3β loop makes minimal contact with the peptide, suggesting that HLA-C engagement and other TCR regions drive recognition. This work is significant for viral immunity and vaccine design because it identifies a conserved nucleocapsid epitope that remains vulnerable across VOC, establishes HLA-C as an important player in controlling SARS-CoV-2 evolution, and provides structural insight into an unconventional TCR-peptide-MHC recognition mode that could inform rational engineering of broadly protective T cell therapies.
